topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Mrs. Albright was humming a little tune as she sorted through the pile of ungraded essays. Sunlight streamed through the dusty windowpanes of her classroom, illuminating the motes dancing in the air. A small, brightly wrapped box landed on her desk, startling her just enough to make her jump. It was addressed simply, and the handwriting was instantly recognizable—the looping, energetic script of Leo Vance, a student from her advanced literature class years ago. Her fingers, usually steady when grading, trembled slightly as she carefully peeled back the wrapping paper. Inside, nestled on tissue paper, was a collection of rare, first-edition poetry books she had only dreamed of owning. A warm, buoyant feeling spread through her chest, chasing away any lingering fatigue from the long school year. She smiled, a wide, genuine curve of her lips, and decided right then and there to spend the afternoon reading by the window.

[story 2]

I hadn't expected the delivery. The mail slot usually offered only junk flyers, but today, there it was: a carefully arranged bouquet of sunflowers, vibrant yellow against the muted grey of the hallway. My shoulders visibly relaxed as I picked it up, the thick stems feeling solid and substantial in my hands. It was from Clara, who used to sit two rows ahead of me in AP History. A genuine, bright warmth flooded my system, making my pace quicken as I walked toward my desk. I looked at the arrangement, then back at the door, a light, almost giddy sensation bubbling up inside me. "Well, isn't this a delightful surprise," I murmured to myself, a soft, pleased sound escaping my lips as I set the flowers down beside my stack of papers.

[story 3]

The sound of the knock on the door was sharp, yet pleasant. I opened it to find Mark, a former student from my remedial writing course, holding out a small, intricately carved wooden bird. His eyes sparkled with a familiar, eager light. A distinct lift occurred in my spirit; the usual weight of administrative tasks seemed to lift momentarily. "This is… unexpected," I managed, my voice lighter than usual. I took the gift, turning the smooth wood over in my palm. A distinct sense of joy settled over me, making my whole posture straighten up. I felt a sudden urge to recount the story of that little bird to the next person who walked by, a buoyant energy propelling me forward.
